name: Synchronization Primitives
summary: The shared-memory coordination layer beneath higher-level concurrency abstractions: locks/mutexes, atomics with memory ordering, condition variables and parking, and lock-free / wait-free data structures used inside schedulers and concurrent containers.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Shared-memory coordination spans a spectrum from low-level locks and atomics to high-level transactional and lock-free abstractions. This record surveys the runtime mechanics — parking discipline, scheduler integration, atomics and memory ordering, and the lock-free queue requirements that schedulers and concurrent containers depend on. Memory-model formalisms belong in `data/ownership` and `data/garbage-collection`; type-level effect tracking lives in `data/type-and-effect-systems`. Software Transactional Memory is treated separately in `data/software-transactional-memory`.

sections.title: Locks and mutexes
sections.content:
Locks are the baseline shared-memory coordination primitive. They are simple, efficient, and compatible with existing code, but are prone to deadlocks, priority inversion, lock convoying, and accidental blocking inside async runtimes.

A language/runtime should decide whether locks block OS threads, park user tasks, integrate with cancellation, or participate in priority inheritance. Tokio's `Mutex` parks the awaiting task; Rust's `std::sync::Mutex` blocks the OS thread; Go's `sync.Mutex` parks the goroutine via `gopark`. Lock implementations also choose between adaptive spinning (CAS for short waits, then OS futex/wait), priority inheritance (Linux PI futexes, RTOS support), and reentrancy (Java's `synchronized` is reentrant; Rust's `Mutex` is not).

sections.title: Atomics and memory ordering
sections.content:
Atomics provide low-level synchronization through memory-ordering rules. They are necessary for runtimes, lock-free queues, reference counts, and high-performance coordination, but they are difficult for application programmers.

C++11 / Rust / C# all expose `relaxed`, `acquire`, `release`, `acq_rel`, `seq_cst` ordering on atomic operations; Java exposes `volatile` plus the more granular `VarHandle` API since JDK 9. The choice of memory ordering is the difference between correct lock-free code and subtle hardware-dependent races. Full memory-model discussion belongs in `data/ownership` and `data/garbage-collection`; this record cares about atomics as scheduler and synchronization building blocks.

sections.title: Condition variables, semaphores, and parking
sections.content:
Condition variables and semaphores block until another thread signals. In user-mode runtimes, these primitives should usually park the logical task rather than the worker thread.

Go's `gopark` and `goready` are runtime primitives for removing and re-adding goroutines to the scheduler run queue. Java virtual threads use a similar mount/unmount mechanism. Rust async typically composes parking from the executor's `Waker` API plus per-resource wait queues (Tokio's `Notify`, `Semaphore`, `RwLock`).

sections.title: Lock-free and wait-free structures
sections.content:
Lock-free queues, deques, and stacks are central to schedulers and executors. They reduce blocking but increase proof burden and expose memory-reclamation challenges. Work-stealing deques (`data/work-stealing#deque-based-scheduling`) are a representative case where owner operations should be cheap while steal operations can afford heavier synchronization.

Memory-reclamation is the load-bearing subtlety: a lock-free reader cannot block a writer from freeing nodes, so the writer needs an external mechanism — hazard pointers, RCU, epoch-based reclamation, QSBR. Each is its own per-tech record (`data/hazard-pointers`, `data/rcu`, `data/epoch-based-reclamation`, `data/qsbr`).

sections.title: Design guidance
sections.content:
A language/runtime designing its synchronization layer should answer:

1. Do locks block the OS thread or park the logical task?
2. Are atomics typed by ordering, and do users see those orderings?
3. Does the runtime expose `park`/`unpark` as a public primitive, or hide it behind higher-level abstractions (channels, futures, mutexes)?
4. Which lock-free structures are part of the standard library, and what reclamation discipline backs them?

The answers shape what library code can be written portably, how easy lock-free programming is for non-expert users, and how well the runtime composes with `cancellation` and `structured-concurrency`.
